raw_response: {"rating": 3.5, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is a news report that consistently frames a scientific consensus document through a skeptical, deregulatory lens, relying almost exclusively on right-leaning and industry-aligned sources to rebut the report's findings. The author's own prose repeatedly signals skepticism toward the NASEM report and support for EPA rollback efforts, supporting a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing describes the NASEM report as something that 'cherry-picks data and stokes climate anxiety' — this editorial characterization appears in the author's connecting prose before any source attribution, presenting a critical interpretation as factual context; (2) The article's source selection is structurally one-sided: all named expert voices come from the Cato Institute, Heritage Foundation, Heartland Institute, and a former Trump EPA official — no scientific, public health, or climate research voices are included to support or contextualize the NASEM findings; (3) The author describes the 2009 Endangerment Finding as 'hastily assembled with potential political motivations' in their own connecting prose, framing established regulatory history through a right-leaning deregulatory lens; (4) The author characterizes the potential EPA rollback in their own voice as something that 'will lower costs and increase freedom for the consumer' — a clearly pro-deregulation editorial framing presented as expert consensus rather than contested opinion; (5) The Heartland Institute, one of the primary sources platformed in this piece, is a fossil-fuel-funded think tank with a documented history of climate change denial campaigns; its director is presented without this context; (6) The author includes the House Oversight Committee investigation into NASEM as a closing note, lending institutional legitimacy to skepticism of the scientific body without noting the investigation's partisan origin or context."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report that consistently frames a scientific consensus document through a skeptical, deregulatory lens, relying almost exclusively on right-leaning and industry-aligned sources to rebut the report's findings. The author's own prose repeatedly signals skepticism toward the NASEM report and support for EPA rollback efforts, supporting a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing describes the NASEM report as something that 'cherry-picks data and stokes climate anxiety' — this editorial characterization appears in the author's connecting prose before any source attribution, presenting a critical interpretation as factual context; (2) The article's source selection is structurally one-sided: all named expert voices come from the Cato Institute, Heritage Foundation, Heartland Institute, and a former Trump EPA official — no scientific, public health, or climate research voices are included to support or contextualize the NASEM findings; (3) The author describes the 2009 Endangerment Finding as 'hastily assembled with potential political motivations' in their own connecting prose, framing established regulatory history through a right-leaning deregulatory lens; (4) The author characterizes the potential EPA rollback in their own voice as something that 'will lower costs and increase freedom for the consumer' — a clearly pro-deregulation editorial framing presented as expert consensus rather than contested opinion; (5) The Heartland Institute, one of the primary sources platformed in this piece, is a fossil-fuel-funded think tank with a documented history of climate change denial campaigns; its director is presented without this context; (6) The author includes the House Oversight Committee investigation into NASEM as a closing note, lending institutional legitimacy to skepticism of the scientific body without noting the investigation's partisan origin or context.

explanation: This is a news report that consistently frames a scientific consensus document through a skeptical, deregulatory lens, relying almost exclusively on right-leaning and industry-aligned sources to rebut the report's findings. The author's own prose repeatedly signals skepticism toward the NASEM report and support for EPA rollback efforts, supporting a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author's own framing describes the NASEM report as something that 'cherry-picks data and stokes climate anxiety' — this editorial characterization appears in the author's connecting prose before any source attribution, presenting a critical interpretation as factual context; (2) The article's source selection is structurally one-sided: all named expert voices come from the Cato Institute, Heritage Foundation, Heartland Institute, and a former Trump EPA official — no scientific, public health, or climate research voices are included to support or contextualize the NASEM findings; (3) The author describes the 2009 Endangerment Finding as 'hastily assembled with potential political motivations' in their own connecting prose, framing established regulatory history through a right-leaning deregulatory lens; (4) The author characterizes the potential EPA rollback in their own voice as something that 'will lower costs and increase freedom for the consumer' — a clearly pro-deregulation editorial framing presented as expert consensus rather than contested opinion; (5) The Heartland Institute, one of the primary sources platformed in this piece, is a fossil-fuel-funded think tank with a documented history of climate change denial campaigns; its director is presented without this context; (6) The author includes the House Oversight Committee investigation into NASEM as a closing note, lending institutional legitimacy to skepticism of the scientific body without noting the investigation's partisan origin or context.
